body: Inspect whether a sticky or fixed-position call-to-action element covers more than 40% of the mobile viewport (viewport.device_type === "mobile"). Sticky CTAs are a common pattern that reinforces the conversion path during scroll, but oversized sticky bars consume reading area, push content below the fold, and obstruct interaction with the underlying page. Positive signals: the sticky CTA bar's height is below 15% of the viewport height; the CTA remains visible during scroll without obscuring more than one full line of body text; the CTA has a visible dismiss affordance. Negative signals: the sticky CTA exceeds 40% viewport coverage on a 375x812 mobile rendering; the CTA expands on scroll without user interaction; the CTA blocks above-fold reading area on first load. When violations are found, recommend reducing the sticky-CTA height to a single-line bar, collapsing detailed content to expand only on tap, and ensuring the sticky element does not push the primary fold content below the viewport. Reference NN/g sticky element overuse guidance which observes that oversized sticky elements degrade mobile reading flow.

provenance.citation_text: Nielsen Norman Group's research on sticky elements observes that elements which occupy more than a small portion of the mobile viewport degrade reading flow and push above-fold content below the screen edge. The guideline recommends keeping sticky bars to single-line height and avoiding expansion on scroll without explicit user gesture.
